shower.thought: We measure our lives in moments we remember, which means the best parts might be the ones we've already forgotten.
You've been dead for brief moments thousands of times—every time you fell asleep without knowing if you'd wake up.
The only reason you can read this is because someone invented a problem (illiteracy) and then sold you the solution (reading).
Your body is constantly replacing itself, which means the person reading this sentence isn't quite the same person who started it.
We call it "falling" asleep but we're actually rising into another state of consciousness.
The people you'll miss most are the ones you'll stop texting first.
You've probably already met your best friend and didn't realize it yet—or already said goodbye to them without knowing.
Every apology you didn't make is still sitting in your chest somewhere.
Mirrors show you what you look like, but only to everyone else. You've never actually seen your own face.
We spend our whole lives running from boredom, yet the moment we stop running, we finally feel alive.
The saddest part about growing up isn't losing your imagination—it's gaining the ability to see why you can't use it anymore.
You can't remember being born, so technically your life is a story that starts partway through.
People who say "I don't care what others think" care very much—just about different people.
Your phone knows you better than you know yourself, which is why its suggestions feel so invasive.
The things you regret saying are usually the truest things you've ever said.
You're nostalgic for a version of your hometown that technically never existed—only your memory of it.
We're all just trying to prove that our life mattered to someone, even if it's just ourselves.
The hardest goodbye is the one where both people want to stay but neither says it.
Every song you love will eventually remind you of a specific moment, and then you can never listen to it the same way again.
You don't actually remember most of your life—you remember the last time you remembered it.

motivational.thought: The master has failed more times than the beginner has even tried. — Stephen McCranie
Your future self is watching you right now through memories you haven't made yet. — Unknown
Discipline is choosing between what you want now and what you want most. — Unknown
The cave you fear to enter holds the treasure you seek. — Joseph Campbell
Most of life isn't about finding the right answer—it's about becoming the kind of person who can handle it. — Unknown
You can't think your way into a new way of acting, but you can act your way into a new way of thinking. — Unknown
Comparison is the thief of contentment and the murderer of motivation. — Unknown
The moment you decide to stop waiting for permission, everything changes. — Unknown
Wounds become wisdom only if you choose to learn from them. — Unknown
Your worst days often teach you more than your best ones ever could. — Unknown
What seems hard now will be the warm-up for what's coming next. — Unknown
Consistency whispers what motivation screams. — Unknown
The difference between who you are and who you want to be is what you do. — Bill Phillips
You are not behind. You are not lost. You are exactly where you need to be to become who you're meant to be. — Unknown
Failure isn't the opposite of success—it's the prerequisite. — Unknown
The life you want is on the other side of the discomfort you're avoiding. — Unknown
Every expert was once a beginner who refused to quit. — Unknown
You don't rise to the level of your goals; you fall to the level of your systems. — James Clear
The only way out is through, and the only way through is to begin. — Unknown
Invest in yourself first. Everything else is secondary income. — Unknown

darkjoke.thought:
I told my girlfriend she'd be perfect if she lost a few pounds.
Turns out I was right, but it took a car accident.

My therapist asked if I was having suicidal thoughts.
I said, "Only on days that end in 'y.'"
I'm not saying my family is toxic, but our reunions have a higher body count than most crime scenes.

My dad always said, "Money can't buy happiness."
He was right—he tried for years.

I got kicked out of the suicide hotline for being too honest.
Apparently "honestly, I think you should just do it" doesn't meet their standards.

My wife asked why I was crying at the funeral.
I said, "Because I'm going to miss him."
She said, "It's your mother."

I asked my boss why he fired me during my mental health crisis.
He said, "We're not equipped for that kind of negativity."
I said, "Fair enough, but does it have to be today?"

My kid asked if Santa was real.
I said yes, then checked his browsing history and realized he knows way more about mortality than I do.

I tried to make a joke about depression, but it didn't go anywhere.
Kind of like my will to live.
I'm on so many medications, my drug dealer called to say he was proud.

My priest said God loves everyone equally.
I pointed to my medical bills and said, "Seems like we have different definitions of love."

I told my son that life isn't fair.
He said, "I know—you got all the happiness."

My girlfriend said she needed space.
I told her to try the kitchen.
Turns out she was serious.
I have a stepladder because my real ladder left when I was just a kid.

My friend said he was at his lowest point.
I said, "Great, now we're finally at eye level."

I asked the pharmacist if antidepressants had side effects.
She said, "Only if they work."

My therapist said I should address my fears head-on.
So I did. Turns out my fear was a mirror.

I'm reading a book on the history of glue.
Can't put it down.

My doctor said I had a year to live.
I couldn't pay the bill.
Turns out that motivated me more than any deadline ever could.

My wife left me because I was too pessimistic.
Good riddance—she would've left eventually anyway.
